23.59% ([[Nielsen ratings]]){{cite news|last=Finke |first=Nikki |title=UPDATE: 39.9 Million Watch 79th Oscars |url=https://deadline.com/2007/02/overnights-show-2-nielsen-oscar-ratings-1489/ |access-date=February 3, 2014 |work=[[Deadline Hollywood]] |publisher=Penske Media Corporation |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131213205642/http://www.deadline.com/2007/02/overnights-show-2-nielsen-oscar-ratings/ |archive-date=December 13, 2013 }} | last = 78th | next = 80th }} The '''79th Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), honored the best [[2006 in film|films of 2006]] and took place February 25, 2007, at the [[Kodak Theatre]] in [[Hollywood, Los Angeles|Hollywood]], [[Los Angeles]] beginning at 5:30 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] / 8:30 p.m. [[Eastern Time Zone|EST]]. During the ceremony, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences presented Academy Awards (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 24 categories. The ceremony, televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], was produced by [[Laura Ziskin]] and directed by Louis J. Horvitz. Actress [[Ellen DeGeneres]] hosted for the first time.{{cite news|title=Ellen, meet Oscar |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2006-09-07-degeneres-host_x.htm |access-date=February 16, 2014 |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=September 7, 2008 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221065805/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2006-09-07-degeneres-host_x.htm |archive-date=February 21, 2014 }} Two weeks earlier in a ceremony at the [[Beverly Wilshire Hotel]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]] held on February 10, the [[Academy Award for Technical Achievement|Academy Awards for Technical Achievement]] were presented by host [[Maggie Gyllenhaal]].{{cite magazine|last=Rich |first=Joshua |title=Maggie G. Hosts Sci/Tech Oscars |url=http://popwatch.ew.com/2007/02/07/maggie_g_hosts_/ |access-date=July 18, 2013 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Inc. |date=February 7, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131202225234/http://popwatch.ew.com/2007/02/07/maggie_g_hosts_/ |archive-date=December 2, 2013 }} ''[[The Departed]]'' won four awards, including [[Academy Award for Best Picture|Best Picture]].{{cite news|last1=Halbfinger |first1=David M. |last2=Waxman |first2=Sharon |title='The Departed' Wins Best Picture, Scorsese Best Director |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/02/26/movies/awardsseason/26osca.html |access-date=February 17, 2014 |work=[[The New York Times]] |date=February 26, 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140304193333/http://www.nytimes.com/2007/02/26/movies/awardsseason/26osca.html |archive-date=March 4, 2014 }}{{cite news|title=At Long Last, Scorsese Wins Oscar |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/at-long-last-scorsese-wins-oscar/ |access-date=October 22, 2013 |work=[[CBS News]] |publisher=[[CBS Corporation]] |date=February 11, 2009 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131022140201/http://www.cbsnews.com/2100-207_162-2511891.html |archive-date=October 22, 2013 }} Other winners included ''[[Pan's Labyrinth]]'' with three awards, ''[[Dreamgirls (film)|Dreamgirls]]'', ''[[An Inconvenient Truth]]'', and ''[[Little Miss Sunshine]]'' with two, and ''[[Babel (film)|Babel]]'', ''[[The Blood of Yingzhou District]]'', ''[[The Danish Poet]]'', ''[[Happy Feet]]'', ''[[The Last King of Scotland (film)|The Last King of Scotland]]'', ''[[Letters from Iwo Jima]]'', ''[[The Lives of Others]]'', ''[[Marie Antoinette (2006 film)|Marie Antoinette]]'', ''[[Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest]]'', ''[[The Queen (2006 film)|The Queen]]'', and ''[[West Bank Story]]'' with one. The telecast garnered nearly 40 million viewers in the United States. ==Winners and nominees== The nominees for the 79th Academy Awards were announced on January 23, 2007, at the [[Samuel Goldwyn Theater]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]], by [[Sid Ganis]], president of the academy, and the actress [[Salma Hayek]].{{cite news|last=Stein |first=Ruthe |title=Sure, the film business is cutthroat, but it also has heart, says industry leader Sid Ganis, who seems to have a lot of heart himself |url=http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/Sure-the-film-business-is-cutthroat-but-it-also-2655088.php |access-date=July 16, 2013 |work=[[The San Francisco Chronicle]] |publisher=[[Hearst Corporation]] |date=January 22, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140202225255/http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/Sure-the-film-business-is-cutthroat-but-it-also-2655088.php |archive-date=February 2, 2014 }} ''[[Dreamgirls (film)|Dreamgirls]]'' received the most nominations with eight, and ''[[Babel (film)|Babel]]'' came in second with seven.{{cite news|last=Kaufman |first=Gil |title='Dreamgirls' Leads Oscar Noms — Without Best Picture Or Beyonce |url=http://www.mtv.com/news/articles/1550536/best-picture-snub-oscar-leader-dreamgirls.jhtml |access-date=February 14, 2014 |work=[[MTV]] |publisher=[[Viacom Media Networks]] |date=January 23, 2007 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222091258/http://www.mtv.com/news/articles/1550536/best-picture-snub-oscar-leader-dreamgirls.jhtml |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} This marked the first and only occurrence that the film with the most nominations was not a Best Picture nominee.{{cite news|last=Rea |first=Steven |title=Oscar hugs and shrugs The Academy Award nominations show unusual diversity this year and, in the case of "Dreamgirls," an oddity. The academy sought ideas to revamp the show while renewing interest with the nominated films. In September 2006, the academy selected producer [[Laura Ziskin]] to oversee production of the telecast for a second time.{{cite news|last=Archerd |first=Army |title=Oscar gig for Ziskin |url=https://variety.com/2006/film/news/oscar-gig-for-ziskin-1200339437/ |access-date=February 3, 2014 |work=Variety |publisher=Penske Media Corporation |date=July 20, 2006 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140303101044/http://variety.com/2006/film/news/oscar-gig-for-ziskin-1200339437/ |archive-date=March 3, 2014 }} Nearly three months later, actress and comedian [[Ellen DeGeneres]], who had previously emceed three [[Primetime Emmy Award]] ceremonies between 2001 and 2005, was chosen as host of the 2007 ceremony. In an article published in the ''[[Los Angeles Times]]'', Ziskin explained the decision to hire DeGeneres saying "Certainly, I believe the presence of Ellen will help the ratings absolutely. She's popular with a very wide audience. She is not a niche performer. She touches a lot of demographics."{{cite news|last=Welkos |first=Robert W. |title=Everybody likes Ellen, especially Oscar |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2006-sep-09-et-ellen9-story.html |access-date=February 3, 2014 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=September 9, 2006 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131222050812/http://articles.latimes.com/2006/sep/09/entertainment/et-ellen9 |archive-date=December 22, 2013 }} AMPAS christened this year's telecast with a theme celebrating movie quotes.{{cite news|title=Movie Quotes Line the Road to Oscars |url=http://oscars.org/press/pressreleases/2007/07.01.25.html |access-date=February 17, 2014 |work=Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences |publisher=AMPAS |date=January 25, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070420220205/http://oscars.org/press/pressreleases/2007/07.01.25.html |archive-date=April 20, 2007 |url-status=live }} In tandem with the theme, advertising agency [[TBWA/Chiat/Day|TBWA\Chiat\Day]] designed the official ceremony poster featuring 75 quotes from several Oscar-nominated or winning films.{{cite news|last=Sneider |first=Jeff |title=Oscar poster lines 'em up |url=https://variety.com/2006/film/news/oscar-poster-lines-em-up-1117956079/ |access-date=February 17, 2014 |work=Variety |publisher=Penske Media Corporation |date=December 19, 2006 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140304224554/http://variety.com/2006/film/news/oscar-poster-lines-em-up-1117956079/ |archive-date=March 4, 2014 }} To stir interest surrounding the awards, filmmaker [[Spike Lee]] released a trailer featuring everyday people around [[New York City]] reciting famous film lines. During the ceremony, a montage produced by director [[Nancy Meyers]] saluted the work of screenwriters and their contributions to film.{{cite news|last=White |first=Dave |title=Oscars: Even more bloated than 'Idol' |url=http://www.today.com/id/17333639/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/oscars-even-more-bloated-idol/ |access-date=February 17, 2014 |work=[[NBC News]] |publisher=[[NBCUniversal]] |date=February 26, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222140453/http://www.today.com/id/17333639/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/oscars-even-more-bloated-idol/ |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }}{{cite news|last=Fernandez |first=Jay A. |title=A step toward recognizing where it starts the nominations announcement on January 23, the combined gross of the five Best Picture nominees was $244 million with an average of $48.7 million per film.{{cite news|title=2006 Academy Award Nominations and Winner for Best Picture |url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2006&p=.htm |access-date=January 30, 2014 |work=[[Box Office Mojo]] |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140131163901/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2006&p=.htm |archive-date=January 31, 2014 }} ''The Departed'' was the highest earner among the Best Picture nominees with $121.7 million in domestic box office receipts. The film was followed by ''Little Miss Sunshine'' ($59.6 million), ''The Queen'' ($35.6 million), ''Babel'' ($23.7 million) and finally ''Letters from Iwo Jima'' ($2.4 million). Of the top 50 grossing movies of the year, 29 nominations went to nine films on the list. Only ''The Pursuit of Happyness'' (12th), ''Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan'' (15th), ''The Devil Wears Prada'' (16th), ''The Departed'' (17th) and ''Dreamgirls'' (28th) were nominated for Best Picture, Best Animated Feature or any of the directing, acting or screenwriting awards.{{cite news|title=2007 Domestic Grosses (as of January 23, 2007) |url=https://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2007-01-23&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2006&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |access-date=April 16, 2007 |work=Box Office Mojo |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140201194935/http://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2007-01-23&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2006&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |archive-date=February 1, 2014 }} The other top 50 box office hits that earned nominations were ''Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest'' (1st), ''Cars'' (2nd), ''Superman Returns'' (6th) and ''Happy Feet'' (8th). ===Critical reviews=== The show received a mixed reception from media publications. Some media outlets were more critical of the show. Tim Goodman of the ''[[San Francisco Chronicle]]'' lamented, "It was long. It was flat. And it was bloated. Worst of all, it was boring." He also wrote that "it was difficult for Ellen's subtle rambling to translate because people want pop and humor and declarative sentences in their Academy Awards. Which they didn't exactly get."{{cite news|last=Goodman |first=Tim |title=Departed evening of bloated, boring Hollywood babble |url=http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/THE-79TH-ACADEMY-AWARDS-Departed-evening-of-2646004.php |access-date=February 6, 2014 |work=[[San Francisco Chronicle]] |publisher=[[Hearst Corporation]] |date=February 25, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222022246/http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/THE-79TH-ACADEMY-AWARDS-Departed-evening-of-2646004.php |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} ''[[The Denver Post]]'' television critic Joanne Ostrow bemoaned, "Pleasant and innocuous but hardly exciting, DeGeneres forgot the primary Academy Award host directive: It's not about the host. Hollywood's biggest night (and television's second-biggest annual gathering, after the [[Super Bowl]]) is a celebration of film."{{cite news|last=Ostrow |first=Joanne |title=Mediocre Ellen lowers telecast to daytime |url=http://www.denverpost.com/opinion/ci_5305942 |access-date=February 6, 2014 |work=The Denver Post |publisher=MediaNews Group |date=February 26, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222062701/http://www.denverpost.com/opinion/ci_5305942 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} ''[[The Washington Post]]'' columnist [[Tom Shales]] gave an average review for DeGeneres but criticized the overall slow and choppy pacing of the program noting that it was "punishingly too long."{{cite news|last=Shales |first=Tom |title=The Broadcast: Long and Longer |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2007/02/26/AR2007022600027.html |access-date=February 6, 2014 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=February 26, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130907082344/http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2007/02/26/AR2007022600027.html |archive-date=September 7, 2013 }} Other media outlets received the broadcast more positively. Columnist [[Alessandra Stanley]] of ''[[The New York Times]]'' lauded DeGeneres's performance writing that she was "cheeky but good-natured, far less barbed and sardonic than [[Jon Stewart]] [[78th Academy Awards|last year]] or [[Chris Rock]] in [[77th Academy Awards|2005]]." She added that her style brought a "casual Friday mood to Fancy Sunday."{{cite news|last=Stanley |first=Alessandra |title=Bringing a Touch of Daytime to Hollywood's Biggest Night |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/02/26/arts/television/26watc.html |access-date=February 3, 2014 |work=The New York Times |date=February 25, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140304193352/http://www.nytimes.com/2007/02/26/arts/television/26watc.html |archive-date=March 4, 2014 }} ''[[St. Louis Post-Dispatch]]'' television critic Gail Pennington praised host DeGeneres and producer Ziskin for turning "the evening into an upbeat celebration––and the most entertaining Oscars in years."{{cite news|last=Pennington|first=Gail|title=DeGeneres kept the Oscar show upbeat and lively|work=[[St. Louis Post-Dispatch]]|publisher=[[Lee Enterprises]]|date=February 26, 2007|page=D1}} Television editor Dave Kronke of the ''[[Los Angeles Daily News]]'' gave high marks for DeGeneres commenting, "Her material was amusing but scarcely a laugh riot, yet it was amiable and delineated that the evening was a celebration of all the nominees, not just the winners."{{cite news|last=Kronke|first=David|title=Yep, She's Funny Gentle Humor wins for DeGeneres|work=[[Los Angeles Daily News]]|publisher=MediaNews Group|date=February 26, 2007|url=http://www.thefreelibrary.com/YEP,+SHE%27S+FUNNY+GENTLE+HUMOR+WINS+FOR+DEGENERES.-a0159975212|access-date=January 6, 2015|archive-date=September 2, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150902221116/http://www.thefreelibrary.com/YEP,+SHE%27S+FUNNY+GENTLE+HUMOR+WINS+FOR+DEGENERES.-a0159975212|url-status=live}} ===Ratings and reception=== The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 39.92 million people over its length, which was a 2.5% increase from the previous year's ceremony.{{Cite web |first=Bill |last=Gorman |title=Academy Awards Averages 41.3 Million Viewers; Most Since 2005 |url=http://tvbythenumbers.com/2010/03/08/academy-awards-averages-41-3-million-viewers-most-since-2005/44217 |publisher=TVbytheNumbers |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=March 12, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100310080531/http://tvbythenumbers.com/2010/03/08/academy-awards-averages-41-3-million-viewers-most-since-2005/44217 |archive-date=10 March 2010 |url-status=dead }}{{cite magazine|last=Rich |first=Joshua |title=A few lingering Oscar curiosities |url=http://popwatch.ew.com/2007/02/27/a_few_lingering/ |access-date=January 31, 2014 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Inc. |date=February 27, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140202095850/http://popwatch.ew.com/2007/02/27/a_few_lingering/ |archive-date=February 2, 2014 }} An estimated 76.72 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards. The show also drew higher [[Nielsen ratings]] compared to the previous ceremony with 23.59% of households watching over a 38.86 share.{{cite news|title=Academy Awards ratings |work=[[Television Bureau of Advertising]] |url=http://www.tvb.org/media/file/Academy_Awards.pdf |access-date=June 27, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130515152338/http://www.tvb.org/media/file/Academy_Awards.pdf |archive-date=May 15, 2013 }} In addition, the program scored a higher 18-49 demo rating with a 14.18 rating over a 33.71 share among viewers in that demographic.{{cite news|last=Gough |first=Paul J. |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/strong-week-abc-but-idol-131032 |title=Strong week for ABC, but 'Idol' lifts Fox |access-date=January 31, 2014 |work=The Hollywood Reporter |publisher=Prometheus Global Media |date=February 28, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140317004719/http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/strong-week-abc-but-idol-131032 |archive-date=March 17, 2014 }} In July 2007, the ceremony presentation received nine nominations at the [[59th Primetime Emmy Awards|59th Primetime Emmys]].{{cite news|title=Primetime Emmy Award Database |url=http://www.emmys.com/shows/79th-annual-academy-awards |access-date=July 18, 2013 |work=[[Academy of Television Arts and Sciences]] |publisher=ATAS |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130623123309/http://www.emmys.com/shows/79th-annual-academy-awards |archive-date=June 23, 2013 }} Two months later, the ceremony won two of those nominations for Outstanding Art Direction (J. Michael Riva, Geoffrey Richman, and Tamlyn Wright) and Outstanding Music Direction (William Ross).{{cite news|title=Emmy Winners List |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/emmy-winners-list/ |access-date=July 17, 2013 |work=CBS News |publisher=CBS Corporation |date=September 16, 2007 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131202235627/http://www.cbsnews.com/news/emmy-winners-list/ |archive-date=December 2, 2013 }}{{cite news|title=The complete list of winners |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2007-sep-17-et-emmywinners17-story.html |access-date=February 1, 2014 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=September 17, 2007 |url-status=live
